content.full_text:
Re
Farn Polrucar- PnacrrcEs CoivrvussroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 . Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
September 23,2003
Janet C. Crocker
37866 Lobelia Drive
Newark, CA 94560
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.I'03-137
Dear Mrs. Crocker:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the'Act').1 We are providing only
informal assistance because there is no specific pending decision before you at this time.
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunityprovided by an
opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114;2 cal. Code of Regs. section
I 8329 (c) (3), copy enclo sed. )
Please note that your letter also raises questions conceming Government Code
section 1090, which is not under the jurisdiction of the Fair Political Practices
Commission. Please consult the Attorney General's office for advice regarding your
obligations under section 1090.
QUESTION
If you are elected as a member of a school district board, may you participate in
and make decisions concerning: 1) employment contracts in which stipends are
negotiated and included in the contracts; 2) reimbursement of expenses incurred by your
husband when acting as a coach for the district's water polo and swimming teams; 3)
field trips by the teams; and 4) approval for fund-raising by or for the teams.
CONCLUSION
Under the Act, you may participate in and make contract decisions affecting the
certificated employees, including your husband, so long as the decision affects all
employees in the same job classificatton in the same manner. However, you may not
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109'18997, of the California Code of Regulations. All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise indicated.
-g-=


File No. I_03_137
Page No. 2
participate in, make, or influence any decisions concerning the hiring, firing, demotion or
disciplining of your husband. Additionally, you may not make, participate in making, or
influence any decision which would set a salary for your husband which is different from
salaries paid to other employees in your husband's job classification or position.
FACTS
You are interested in running for the Newark Unified School District Board of
Education in Novernber. Your husband works for the district as a coach of the boys'
waterpolo and boys' swimming teams, and he earns a stipend for each sport.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or
attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which he or she has a financial interest. Section 87103 states that apublic official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on
any of his or her economic interests. To identitz disqualifuing conflicts of interest, the
Commission has developed an eight-step analysis, set out in regulation 18700(b). We
apply this analysis to your circumstances.
steps 1 and 2: Are You a Public official who will be Making, participating in
Making, or Influencing a Governmental Decision?
A public official is "any metnber, officer, employee or consultant of a state or
local government agency," including, but not limited to, paid or unpaid members of
boards or commissions with decisionmaking authority. (Section 82}49,regulation
18701.) If you win election to the board, you will be a public official for purposes of the
Act.
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only where a public official
"make[s], participate[s] in making or in any way attempt[s] to use his [or her] official
position to influence a govemmental decision in which he [or she] knows or has reason to
know he [the official] has a financial interest." (Section 87100; regulation 18700(b)(2).)
The commission has adopted a series of regulations which define "making,"
"participating in making," and o'influencing" a governmental decision, and which also
provide certain exceptions. (Regulations I 8702- I S7 02.4.)
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation18702.1.) If you were to vote on a
matter affecting the office of district administrator, you would be making a governmental
decision.


File No. I-03-137
Page No. 3
A public official "participates in a govemmental decision" when, acting within
the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or intervening
review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recoflrmendations to the decisionmaker
regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.)If yortnegotiate, advise, or
otherwise recommend to the decisionmaker(s) that the board should take certain action
regarding the office of dishict administrator, you would be participating in a
governmental decision
A public official "is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision il for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts, or appears
before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee or consultant
of the agency." (Regulation 18702.3.) If you were to contact any board member or
employee of the board for the purpose of influencing their decision regarding a possible
vote or application of the office of district administrator's, than you would be influencing
the governmental decision.
Step 3: What are the Applicable Economic Interests?
If the governmental decision at issue will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on your economic interests, you may have a conflict of interest. you must
therefore identify your economic interests. There are six kinds of economic interests from
which conflicts may arise. They are defined in section 87103 and regulations 18703-
18703.5. Two of these economic interests are implicated by your question, they are:
. Any source of income to the public official which aggregates to $500 or more within
12 months prior to the decision. (section 87103(c), Regulation 1s703.3.)
o A public official also has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family (the
o6personal financial effects" rule). (Section 87103, Regulation 1g703.5.)
Note that the Act distinguishes the flow of tncome fromits source. A public official
generally has a separate economic interest in each.
Source of Income: Obviously, an employer is a source of income in the form of
salary and benefits. Your spouse is employedby aschool in the district and you
presumably have a community property interest in his income. (Section 82030)
However, the Act's definition of income expressly excludes "[s]alary and reimbursement
for expenses orper diem received from a. . . local . . . government agency . . . .,, (Section
82030@)(2).) Thus, where the employer is a local govemmental agency, there is
typically no economic interest.
Personal Financial Effect: In addition to the economic interests separately listed
in section 87103, a public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal
finances, and may have a conflict of interest in any decision foreseeably resulting in an


File No. I-03-131
Page No. 4
increase or decrease in the personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities of the official
or his or her immediate family. (Regulation 18703.5.)
Steps 4-6: \MiII Your Economic fnterest be Directly or fndirectly Involved in the
Decision and is a Material Financial Effect on Your Economic Interest Reasonably
Foreseeable?
Regulation 18704.5 provides: "A public official or his or her immediate family
are deemed to be directly involved in a govemmental decision which has any financiai
effect on his or her personal finances or those of his or her immediate family." For
purposes of analyzing personal financial effects, generally an official will not have a
conflict of interest based on a decision that affects only the salary, per diem, or
reimbursement for expenses the official receives from a local governmental agency.
(Regulation 18705.) Therefore, a decision affecting an official's govemmental salary will
not generally be a problem under this rule, 'lrnless the decision is to hire, fire, promote,
demote, suspend without pay or otherwise take disciplinary action with financial sanction
against the official or a member of his or her immediate family, or to set a salary for the
official or a member of his or her immediate family which is different from salaries paid
to other employees of the government agency in the same job classification or position.,,
(Regulation I 8705.s(b).)
As noted above, without knowing the specific facts regarding a decision with
which you will be confronted, we cannot give you anymore specific advice.
Steps 7 and.8: 66Public Generally" and'ol,egally Required ParticipationD Exceptions
Step seven is an exception that applies where the reasonably foreseeable and
mateial financial effect on the official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. Step eight is an exception that applies when the official is
legally required to participate in the decision. Your facts do not suggest either of these
exceptions is at issue.
Should you be elected to the board and if you have any other further questions
regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel
W. Wallace
t General Counsel
Enclosure

Division

CONCLUSION: Under the Act, you may participate in and make contract decisions affecting the

employees in the same job classificatton in the same manner. However, you may not
